definition: ProLiant form factors: DL = rack-mount (density/general), ML = tower (offices/edge), XL = high-density nodes for chassis like Apollo/Cray. The trailing number/letters denote size and socket count.
HPE's embedded server management processor (BMC). Provides remote console, power control, health monitoring, firmware management and secure recovery independent of the host OS.
HPE security feature that anchors firmware integrity in the iLO silicon. Cryptographically verifies firmware from boot, detects tampering, and enables automated recovery to a known-good state.
HPE's edge-to-cloud platform delivering on-prem infrastructure as a consumption-based (pay-per-use) service, with cloud-style provisioning, metering, and a unified management console.

Cloud-based, GreenLake-delivered service for managing ProLiant servers at scale: firmware/driver updates, monitoring, and automation from a single SaaS console — successor direction to on-prem OneView for compute.
On-premises infrastructure management platform that uses software-defined templates and a unified API to automate provisioning, firmware, and lifecycle across HPE servers, storage, and networking.
Composable infrastructure platform: pools of compute, storage, and fabric that software can compose on demand into right-sized resources via a single API — blurring the line between blade and rack.

Scale-up x86 servers supporting very large shared-memory footprints (many sockets, tens of TB RAM) for in-memory databases (e.g., SAP HANA) and mission-critical workloads needing a single large system image.
HPE's flagship exascale supercomputer platform (from the Cray acquisition). Liquid-cooled, blade-based, using Slingshot interconnect; powers systems like Frontier and El Capitan.
Dense, accelerator-optimized HPC/AI servers (from Cray) for GPU-heavy training clusters — high node density with support for NVIDIA HGX and similar accelerator trays.
HPE Cray's high-performance Ethernet-based HPC interconnect with advanced congestion control and adaptive routing; the fabric behind top exascale systems, positioned against InfiniBand.
Turnkey, co-engineered HPE + NVIDIA solution (part of 'NVIDIA AI Computing by HPE') bundling GreenLake, ProLiant/accelerated compute, storage, and the NVIDIA AI stack for fast on-prem AI deployment.
HPE's disaggregated, all-flash storage platform with independently scalable compute and capacity, delivered as block or file and managed through the cloud (GreenLake / Data Services).
Ruggedized, compact servers built to run compute and analytics at the edge — factories, telco, remote sites — where full data-center environments aren't available.

HPE's virtualization and hybrid-cloud management software (from the Morpheus Data acquisition), including a KVM-based hypervisor option positioned as a lower-cost alternative amid VMware licensing changes.
Fault-tolerant systems for continuous-availability transaction processing (banking, payments, telco). Redundant, shared-nothing architecture designed to never lose a transaction.
2U 1S AMD. Compact, edge-focused rack server aimed at distributed locations that need strong core performance, GPU options, and remote lifecycle management.
1U 1S Intel. Space-efficient rack server for general virtualization, web, and branch workloads where density and power efficiency are priorities.
1U 1S AMD. Rack platform positioned for virtualization and scale-out workloads that benefit from high core counts and strong performance per watt.
2U 1S Intel. Balanced rack server class for mixed enterprise workloads that need more expansion and thermal headroom than compact 1U designs.
2U 1S AMD. Server for storage-rich and expansion-heavy deployments, often used for virtual infrastructure and data-intensive services.
1U 2S Intel. Mainstream enterprise rack server optimized for high-density virtualization, databases, and business applications in constrained rack space.
1U 2S AMD. Server for dense virtualization and cloud-scale workloads needing strong core density in minimal rack units.
2U 2S Intel. Flagship general-purpose rack server known for broad configurability, expansion options, and fit across virtualization, database, and infrastructure roles.
4U 2S Intel. Accelerator-optimized DL380 variant for AI and GPU-heavy workloads, adding power, cooling, and layout choices tuned for accelerators.
2U 2S AMD. Enterprise server used for virtualization and data platform workloads that value high memory bandwidth and scalable I/O.
2U 4S Intel. Dense x86 server for scale-up consolidation where many cores, large memory capacity, and high availability are needed in limited rack space.
4U 4S Intel. High-end enterprise platform for large in-memory databases and mission-critical virtualization requiring major RAM and I/O expansion.
Tower 1S Intel. Entry platform for SMB and branch offices, typically used for file/print, collaboration, and light virtualization.
Tower 1S Intel. Platform offering more headroom than entry models for growing SMB workloads, including business apps and modest virtualization.
Tower 2S Intel. Mainstream server for branch and midmarket environments needing enterprise features, large memory/storage options, and expansion flexibility.
Composable module 2S Intel. Compute node in the Synergy platform for template-driven provisioning and pooled infrastructure for private cloud.
Composable module 4S Intel. Synergy compute module for memory-intensive and scale-up workloads that benefit from composable infrastructure operations.
Chassis system; node U/S varies; Intel or AMD by node. High-density shared infrastructure for scale-out compute and HPC-style workloads.
Chassis system; node U/S varies; Intel or AMD by node. Data-centric high-density family tuned for storage-heavy and big-data use cases.
